sector: Electrical Equipment — custom-engineered MV/LV switchgear, integrated power-control rooms, bus duct & behind-the-meter power distribution for data centers, electric utility, LNG/oil & gas, petrochemical and traction.
bull_thesis: Powell Industries is one of the cleanest small/mid-cap pure plays on the electrical-distribution bottleneck of the AI build-out, and the proof is in orders, not storytelling. In its fiscal second quarter of 2026 (the quarter ended 3/31/26), Powell booked orders of $490M, up 97% from a year earlier, a 1.7x book-to-bill (new orders divided by revenue, so it is booking work faster than it ships), and a backlog of $1.8B, up 33% year over year and 12% from the prior quarter, with visibility well into fiscal 2028. After the quarter closed, on 5/4/26, Powell won a greenfield (brand-new, built-from-scratch) data center award worth more than $400M, the largest in company history, for behind-the-meter power infrastructure (power supplied on-site rather than drawn from the grid, including on-site generation and power islands), with further internal phases under pursuit and a roughly two-year build through fiscal 2028. The strategic upgrade is that Powell is moving up the value chain from simply connecting customers to the utility toward building behind-the-meter power islands, exactly as the queues to connect to the grid stall, which widens its addressable market. Demand is broadening, not narrowing: in the same quarter Commercial and Other Industrial grew 35% and Electric Utility 14%, more than offsetting a 37% decline in petrochemical, so the reduced-cyclicality case is being earned in real time. The balance sheet is a fortress: about $545M in cash and short-term investments, zero debt, $51M of quarterly operating cash flow, $709M of equity, and return on invested capital of roughly 35% in fiscal 2025. Copper is hedged to lock in the margins on the backlog. There is also optionality the market is not paying for: a new defense and government funnel (the REMSDAQ business plus a Defense Production Act Section 303 designation that makes electrical products defense-essential) and a multi-year build-out tied to LNG exports. Fiscal 2025 already delivered the step-up the earlier story implied, with revenue rising from $1.0B to $1.1B, gross margin improving from 27.0% to 29.4%, and earnings per share up 12% to $4.22. If Powell green-lights the $70-100M greenfield expansion (adding $100-250M of revenue capacity) and converts even half of the backlog jump, earnings per share can compound at double-digit rates through fiscal 2027-2028 off a high-return, net-cash base, and the multiple can hold because the business is structurally less cyclical than the market remembers. This depends on the greenfield being sanctioned and built on time, the skilled-labor and floor-space constraint being solved, the data center award executing without phase delays, and the AI and LNG capital-spending cycle staying favorable through fiscal 2028.
bear_thesis: The demand is real, but the price has already paid for it. Powell trades around $299 (about a $10.8B market value) at roughly 28-30 times forward earnings and about 17-25 times EV/EBITDA (enterprise value, the market value plus net debt, divided by earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization) after a near-vertical re-rating; its 52-week range runs from $56.70 to $328, it rose 10% on the second-quarter report, and it sits near all-time highs. The headline catalyst, the more-than-$400M data center award, came after the quarter and is fully public, so the re-rating it set off has largely happened, and you would be buying after the news. The fundamentals carry a warning the headline hides: despite 6% revenue growth, earnings per share actually fell about 1% year over year, from $1.27 to $1.25, as higher compensation, research and development at 1.4% of revenue, and selling, general and administrative expense (up $4M, from REMSDAQ) ate the growth. The orders are not yet converting to the bottom line, and the multiple assumes they will, fast. That conversion is capped by a hard physical ceiling management openly admits: floor space and skilled labor. The $70-100M greenfield expansion that the fiscal 2027-2028 capacity bridge depends on is unsanctioned, described only as a decision within the next few quarters; quick $8M fab spending and 50,000-square-foot leases are stopgaps. Margin is near a cyclical high and flat-to-down year over year (29.6%, off 30 basis points), with about 90 basis points of the quarter-over-quarter gain coming from one-time project close-outs rather than the run rate. The moat is narrow (built on engineering and qualification, not patents), and management itself flags intensifying competition: a lot of new entrants, some new private equity money coming in, which means capacity will chase this demand and compress returns. The marquee award is a single project with a single, undisclosed counterparty, so a phase delay or cancellation is a real tail risk. And the entire case rides the same shared macro factor, the durability of AI capital spending, as the rest of Basket B (the GRID group), so a guide-down in AI spending would pull the whole group down at once. At 30 times earnings, any conversion stumble, capacity miss, or wobble in the capital-spending cycle re-rates this hard. The signs that would confirm the bear case: a quarter where earnings still do not drop through from the backlog, a greenfield that is delayed or over budget, an AI capital-spending scare, or competitive pricing showing up in the book-to-bill.
sop_bull: $330-360 per share. This is a whole-company estimate of intrinsic worth, because Powell is reported by end-market rather than as separable segments that would justify a true sum-of-the-parts (valuing each business line on its own). For the core operating business, apply a premium-but-justified roughly 30 times multiple to forward earnings power of about $6.0-6.5 per share (a normalized fiscal 2027, reflecting backlog conversion, the data center award build, and the LNG and utility ramp on a 1.7x book-to-bill), which gives about $180-195 per share on earnings times multiple. Alternatively, apply roughly 16-18 times EV/EBITDA to about $280-300M of forward EBITDA on the $1.8B-plus backlog, which is roughly $290-315 per share of enterprise value. Add the net-cash overlay of about $545M (around $15 per share) with zero debt. Add the unpriced optionality, the defense and government funnel, the multi-year LNG work, and the greenfield capacity (adding $100-250M of revenue), credited at about $20-35 per share. As a cross-check against the group, Powell at about 30 times sits below GE Vernova (about 71 times forward) and nVent (about 38 times forward) and roughly in line with Eaton (about 32 times) and Hubbell (about 29 times) despite the cleanest book-to-bill, which supports the upper-bound multiple. The bull estimate works out to about $330-360 per share.
sop_bear: $180-215 per share. Bring the multiple down toward the bottom of the peer group and the earnings down toward the run rate. Apply a lowered roughly 22-24 times multiple (a switchgear contractor with a narrow moat, cyclical end-markets, and flat-to-down year-over-year earnings does not deserve a secular-compounder multiple) to a more conservative roughly $5.0-5.5 of normalized earnings per share (backlog converts more slowly; the one-time close-outs and pricing tailwinds fade; compensation, R&D and SG&A keep eating into growth), which gives about $110-130 per share on earnings. Alternatively, apply roughly 12-13 times EV/EBITDA to about $240-260M of EBITDA, which is roughly $150-175 per share of enterprise value. Add net cash of about $15 per share. Cut the data center award optionality (single undisclosed counterparty, phase risk) to near zero. The bear estimate works out to about $180-215 per share; even the bear case is not catastrophic, because of the net-cash, high-return base. It is a de-rating from a stretched multiple, not a value-trap impairment.
sop_breakdown: Base-case whole-company intrinsic worth is about $255 per share, built from roughly 25 times a blended forward earnings power of about $5.6-6.0 (about $140-170 on earnings), cross-checked at roughly 14-15 times EV/EBITDA on about $265M of forward EBITDA (about $215-235 of enterprise value), plus about $15 per share of net cash, plus about $15-20 per share credited but discounted for the defense, LNG and greenfield optionality. This reconciles the bull estimate of $330-360 and the bear estimate of $180-215 toward the midpoint, deliberately tilted down from the bull because the load-bearing data center catalyst has already fired, the multiple is near the high end of the peer group, and earnings conversion is still unproven (earnings per share fell 1% year over year on 6% higher revenue). At about $299 the stock is roughly 17% above this base-case fair value, so the AI scale-out optionality is largely priced in.
risk_reward: Unfavorable-to-balanced from $299. The downside to where the bear case breaks (bear estimate of about $180-215, midpoint about $200) is roughly 33% lower; the move to base-case fair value ($255) is about 15% lower, meaning the stock already sits above base-case fair value; and the upside to the bull case ($330-360, midpoint about $345) is about 15%. That is roughly a 1-to-0.5 reward-to-risk against the base case, so you are paying up for a catalyst that has already fired. The skew only turns attractive on a meaningful pullback (toward about $220-240) or on a fresh, hard-dated catalyst (the greenfield being sanctioned plus a second hyperscaler award).
catalyst_status: FIRED. The load-bearing catalyst — the >$400M greenfield data center award, 'largest in company history' — was announced 5/4/26 (post-Q2), is fully public, and drove a ~+10% move into a stock near all-time highs ($328 52-wk high vs $299 now). The order/backlog inflection (orders +97%, book-to-bill 1.7x, backlog $1.8B) is also already reported and in the price. The DC award is a single greenfield first-phase with an undisclosed counterparty and a ~2-year burn — already-printed, not a forward asymmetric event. The only PENDING_HARD items are second-order: the $70–100M greenfield-capacity sanction ('within the next few quarters') and any FOLLOW-ON internal-phase / second-hyperscaler award — neither yet dated or binding. Net status: FIRED on the primary catalyst; the re-rate is largely spent.
peer_comps_note: The true peers are the data center and grid electrical-distribution group (the scale-out Basket B GRID layer): GE Vernova (GEV, grid plus on-site power, about 71 times forward price/earnings, the group's richest), nVent (NVT, switchgear plus data center enclosures and power, about 38 times forward price/earnings, with 26-28% '26 sales guidance on data center demand), Eaton (ETN, electrical and data center power, about 32 times forward price/earnings), and Hubbell (HUBB, transmission and distribution plus utility, about 28-29 times price/earnings); secondary mega-cap references are Schneider, ABB, Vertiv, and Mitsubishi Electric. Where Powell ranks, with multiples verified live in June 2026: on valuation, it is mid-group at about 28-30 times forward price/earnings and about 17-25 times EV/EBITDA, cheaper than GE Vernova and nVent and in line with Eaton and Hubbell, but it is the smallest and most project- and end-market-concentrated, so it should arguably trade at a slight discount to the diversified mega-caps, not in line. On growth, it is best-in-group on the leading indicator (orders up 97%, book-to-bill 1.7x, backlog up 33%) but only 6% on reported revenue (the conversion lag); nVent's guided revenue growth of 26-28% is currently higher on the income statement. On margins, gross margin of about 29.6% is solid for an engineering-procurement-flavored switchgear maker but below the diversified-product peers (Eaton and Hubbell run higher operating margins); Powell is more project- and contract-margin in character. On returns, return on invested capital of about 35% in fiscal 2025 is excellent and at or above the group, flattered by the cyclical wave. On the balance sheet, Powell is the cleanest: zero debt plus about $545M net cash versus leveraged peers (Eaton, nVent and Hubbell carry net debt). On AI-capital-spending exposure, it is high and direct (behind-the-meter data center power) but more concentrated and binary than the diversified peers, so it has higher sensitivity to the single AI-capital-spending factor. Net: Powell is the high-quality, net-cash, best-book-to-bill name in the group, trading at a fair-to-full multiple that does not obviously discount its smaller scale and single-factor concentration.
role_in_scaleout: Powell is a behind-the-meter, outside-the-box electrical-distribution supplier: custom medium- and low-voltage switchgear, integrated power-control rooms, bus duct, and on-site power-island infrastructure that sits between the utility (or on-site generation) and the data hall, a hard physical chokepoint every hyperscaler and sovereign-AI capital-spending dollar must pass through to energize a campus. Demand durability is high near-term (fiscal 2028 backlog visibility, a 1.7x book-to-bill, and LNG, utility and data center legs) but cyclical, not annuity-like: it is gated by the AI-capital-spending and electrification capital-spending cycle and by Powell's own capacity to build, with new entrants and private equity money signaling a competitive supply response over time.
consensus_delta: Where we disagree with the market is that the street is not meaningfully offside, and that is the problem. After the 97% jump in orders and the more-than-$400M data center award, sell-side analysts and the tape have fully embraced the grid-bottleneck, reduced-cyclicality, AI-power-compounder story; the stock is up fivefold from its 52-week low ($56.70 to $299) and trades at roughly 28-30 times forward earnings. The gap between our view and the market is therefore thin to negative: the market is extrapolating the 1.7x book-to-bill into a smooth fiscal 2027-2028 earnings ramp and granting Powell a near-secular-compounder multiple in line with diversified mega-caps (Eaton about 32 times, Hubbell about 29 times) even though Powell is smaller, more project-concentrated, and more exposed to a single factor (AI capital spending). The genuinely under-appreciated facts cut both ways. In Powell's favor, the defense and government leg (the Defense Production Act Section 303 designation) and the LNG leg, plus the behind-the-meter market expansion, are not fully in the numbers. Against it, the street is under-weighting that earnings per share fell 1% year over year on 6% higher revenue (a conversion lag), that about 90 basis points of the margin beat was one-time close-outs, and that the greenfield capacity needed to convert the backlog is unsanctioned. The mistaken consensus assumption is that orders equal near-term earnings; Powell just demonstrated the opposite. With the catalyst fired and the multiple full, there is no obvious mispricing to harvest at $299.
valley_of_death: Powell has no balance-sheet risky window: zero debt, about $545M of net cash, $51M of quarterly operating cash flow, no wall of maturing debt, and no cash-burn hump. The near-term risk over the next three to nine months is to the stock, not to solvency. First, conversion-lag prints: the next one or two quarters can again show revenue and earnings lagging the backlog (the 97% jump in orders will not hit the income statement for several quarters given the roughly two-year data center build), and at 30 times earnings any in-line-but-not-accelerating quarter can de-rate the stock 15-25%. Second, the capacity overhang from the unsanctioned $70-100M greenfield: a delay, an over-budget sanction, or visible skilled-labor constraints would directly challenge the fiscal 2027-2028 bridge. Third, margin normalization: losing the roughly 90 basis points of one-time close-out tailwind and any pricing give-back as new entrants compete. Fourth, the shared-factor tail: a guide-down in AI capital spending from a hyperscaler (the regime note flags AI capital spending as roughly 40% of 2026 S&P earnings growth and the dominant two-sided risk) would pull the entire GRID group down at once, and Powell's high single-factor exposure means it falls hardest. Fifth, the fiscal first-quarter 2026 Iran/oil precedent (the VIX volatility index spiking from 0 to 26 in days) shows the tape's tail is live. None of these impair the underlying worth, but all can punish a 30-times stock before the fiscal 2028 backlog converts.
positioning_washout: There is no mechanical-selling setup and no forced-selling pressure to wait for, but there is also no capitulated base to buy into. Powell pays only a token dividend ($0.27 per share, $6.5M over 6 months), so there is no yield-focused holder base to flush out on a cut, and no change that would force liquidation. The 3-for-1 stock split (4/6/26) and the increase in authorized shares to 90M improved liquidity and likely broadened the holder base (retail and momentum investors) rather than concentrating it, which cuts the other way: a momentum and growth holder base near all-time highs is prone to mechanical selling on any AI capital-spending scare or growth-factor unwind, not to forced buying. The name screens as a momentum and quality-growth holding, not a special situation with a positioning vacuum to exploit. The wash-out to wait for, if any, is a broad AI-capital-spending or growth-factor sell-off that takes Powell back toward its base-case fair value (about $255) or below. That is the entry, not now.
forcing_function: No single hard-dated re-rating event is pending in the near window; the primary trigger (the more-than-$400M data center award) already fired on 5/4/26. The forward dated items are softer and second-order. First, fiscal third-quarter 2026 earnings (around early August 2026), the first read on whether the backlog is starting to convert to revenue and earnings and whether margins hold without the close-outs; this is the next make-or-break print. Second, the greenfield-capacity sanction decision, which management has guided to within the next few quarters; a sanction (with a second hyperscaler award attached) would be a genuine new catalyst, while a deferral would pressure the multiple. Third, any follow-on internal phase or second data center award, undated but the real bull catalyst. Fourth, full fiscal 2026 results (November 2026) for the complete conversion picture. None is a binding, dated, asymmetric event today, which is exactly why the name is a watchlist candidate rather than an aggressive entry.
moderator_conclusion: Powell is a genuinely high-quality business at a full-to-stretched price with its load-bearing catalyst already fired, the textbook great-company, late-entry profile, and the current market regime makes that worse, not better. Under the Catalyst Watch regime (2026-06-05), the regime explicitly penalizes catalysts that have already fired or already run and penalizes concentration in the single AI-capital-spending factor, and it flags AI capital spending as the dominant shared macro driver under the tape (roughly 40% of 2026 S&P earnings growth), which calls for resolution drivers that are independent of one another at the book level. Powell fails on both counts: its more-than-$400M data center award has fired (the re-rating is spent, the stock rose 10% on the print and sits near all-time highs after a fivefold move off the lows), and its entire case rides the same AI-capital-spending factor as the rest of Basket B (GRID), the opposite of independence. The forensic quality is real and corroborated (book-to-bill 1.7x, backlog $1.8B up 33% with fiscal 2028 visibility, return on invested capital about 35%, zero debt, about $545M net cash, credibility 4 and strengthening), but at about $299 the stock trades roughly 17% above the base-case fair value (about $255) and at about 28-30 times forward earnings, in line with diversified mega-cap peers despite being smaller and more concentrated. Earnings per share actually fell 1% year over year on 6% higher revenue: the orders are not yet converting, and the multiple already prices the conversion as done. Risk and reward from here is roughly 1-to-0.5 against the base case (15% down to fair value, 15% up to the bull case, 33% down to the bear case). The verdict is to pass for now at this price, with conviction 3. This is not a value-trap pass: the moat is narrow but stable, the secular setup (electrification and AI power) is a tailwind, and the balance sheet is pristine, so the value conviction is also 3, not lower. Stripped of the catalyst and regime overlay, the business is high-quality but trades modestly above intrinsic fair value, so the pure-value case is fair rather than compelling. Re-engage on a meaningful pullback toward about $220-240 (a broad AI-capital-spending or growth-factor sell-off is the most likely trigger), or on a new hard catalyst, the greenfield sanction paired with a second hyperscaler award. This is a watchlist name, not a buy.
interrogator_dossier:
# Powell Industries (POWL) — Forensic Dossier

*A deep dive on management and the setup, for scale-out Basket B (GRID). Latest data: fiscal second quarter of 2026 (the quarter ended 3/31/26), reported 5/4/26; full-year fiscal 2025 (ended 9/30/25). Price about $299 (after a 3-for-1 split effective 4/6/26), market value about $10.8B.*

## 1. Narrative arc
Powell has cleanly transformed from a cyclical oil-and-gas and petrochemical-levered switchgear contractor into one of the cleanest small/mid-cap pure plays on the electrical-distribution bottleneck of the AI build-out. The pivot is evidence-backed: from fiscal 2024 to fiscal 2025, revenue stepped up from $1.0B to $1.1B, gross margin from 27.0% to 29.4%, and earnings per share from $3.77 to $4.22 on LNG, data center and utility demand. By the second quarter of fiscal 2026 the story re-anchored on data centers and behind-the-meter power, marked by the more-than-$400M greenfield data center award (5/4/26), the largest project award in company history, plus new defense and government legs (REMSDAQ and the Defense Production Act Section 303 designation) and an LNG leg. The arc is one of expansion, with no drivers dropped.

## 2. Claims versus financials
Revenue grew 6% to $296.6M, which matches the claims but lags the 97% jump in orders; the backlog is the story, not the income statement. Demand is broad (Commercial up 35%, Utility up 14%, oil and gas up 11%), but petrochemical down 37% shows the legacy volatility. Orders of $490M, a 1.7x book-to-bill, and a backlog of $1.8B (up 33%) into fiscal 2028 are strongly supported and the load-bearing fact. Gross margin of 29.6% (down 30 basis points year over year, up 120 basis points quarter over quarter, of which about 90 basis points were one-time close-outs) is honest, not hyped, and near a cyclical high. The tension: earnings per share fell about 1% (from $1.27 to $1.25) despite 6% higher revenue (compensation plus R&D at 1.4% plus an SG&A drag), so growth is not yet converting. The fortress balance sheet ($545M cash, zero debt, $51M operating cash flow, return on invested capital about 35%) is fully supported. Capacity (the unsanctioned $70-100M greenfield) is the gating risk. Net: the narrative is richer than the income statement and poorer than the backlog, in transit.

## 3. Tone and confidence
Rising: confident but disciplined, not promotional. Offensive on market size (the behind-the-meter market is going up, beyond a straight utility connect; the largest award in history; LNG as a multi-year build-out) yet governed by execution realism (a two-year build, capacity equals floor space plus talent, we engage them on sequencing). Candid on competition (much more competitive, new entrants, some new private equity money).

## 4. Guidance credibility
Medium-high. Powell gives light formal guidance (capital spending, R&D as a percent), so there is less to miss; what it does commit to lands (the fiscal 2025 margin and earnings step-up was delivered; capital spending of $12-13M was specific; R&D at 1.4% landed in the 1-1.5% band). Watch: there is no revenue-conversion guide on the $1.8B backlog beyond visibility into fiscal 2028.

## 5. Analyst pressure points
Backlog conversion and capacity (can you build it? — honest but unresolved, with the greenfield unsanctioned); margin durability (management volunteered that about 90 basis points was one-time close-outs); data center concentration and the undisclosed counterparty (data centers are low-twenties of the 29% commercial backlog); pricing (narrowed to supply-constrained product areas, with no broad pricing-power claim).

## 6. Red and green flags
Green: backlog $1.8B (up 33%), book-to-bill 1.7x, fiscal 2028 visibility. Green: net cash about $545M, zero debt, return on invested capital about 35%, $51M operating cash flow. Green: the more-than-$400M behind-the-meter data center award expands the addressable market. Green: real diversification (Commercial up 35% versus petrochemical down 37%). Green: defense and LNG optionality added. Red: earnings per share fell about 1% year over year on 6% higher revenue. Red: capacity is the hard ceiling, with the greenfield unsanctioned. Red: competition intensifying (new entrants, private equity money). Red: the marquee catalyst has fired (post-quarter, public, up 10% on the print). Red: single-project, undisclosed-counterparty concentration.

## 7. Hidden signals
The shift in language to behind-the-meter and power island (moving up the value chain as the queues to connect to the grid stall). Reduced cyclicality repeated as a thesis, with management re-rating its own multiple narrative. A defense and government recurring-revenue funnel being seeded. Copper hedged as insurance to protect backlog margins, which locks in the conversion economics. The 3-for-1 split plus 90M shares authorized signals confidence and liquidity plus capacity and acquisition flexibility.

## 8. Capital-allocation verdict
Trajectory: strengthening. Powell turned a cyclical switchgear business into a genuine grid and behind-the-meter AI-power beneficiary (1.7x book-to-bill, backlog up 33% to fiscal 2028, a record more-than-$400M award, return on invested capital about 35%, zero debt and $545M net cash). The single most important thing to weigh: this is a backlog-conversion and capacity story whose marquee catalyst has already fired into a stock near all-time highs at about 28-30 times forward earnings, so demand is real and durable, but how much is already paid for, and can Powell physically build it (the greenfield is unsanctioned)? The moat is narrow and stable: intangible and qualification-based (custom safety-critical medium-voltage switchgear, long qualification cycles, specification switching costs), real but not patent, network, or scale-based; return on invested capital is high and rising (a widening tell), but gross margin is flat-to-down year over year and competition is intensifying, which caps it at narrow and stable, with returns cyclically elevated and unproven through a downturn. The secular threat is manageable and largely a tailwind (a picks-and-shovels supplier to electrification and AI power, with no displacement or disintermediation risk); the genuine risks are cyclicality (the AI-capital-spending, LNG and petrochemical capital-spending cycle) and a competitive supply response, which are cyclical and competitive, not terminal. The cheapness question is whether the AI-capital-spending optionality is already priced in, not whether this is a value trap.

Management credibility 4 of 5; trajectory strengthening; narrow moat; moat trend stable; secular threat manageable.
skeptic_kill_fact: No fact was found that breaks the bear or pass case; every load-bearing claim verifies against the primary 8-K press release. The bear case (the catalyst has fired, demand is priced in, earnings are not converting) is independently confirmed: fiscal second-quarter 2026 earnings per share fell to $1.25 from $1.27 the prior year on 6% higher revenue ($296.6M); the more-than-$400M data center award came after the quarter (announced 5/4/26) and is fully public, and the re-rating has already fired (split-adjusted 52-week high of $328.00 on 5/11/26, now about $293). The pass verdict survives the check.
skeptic_corrections: Minor: the $255 fair value and the bear earnings and margin claims are built on the latest reported quarter (fiscal second quarter 2026, ended 3/31/26, reported 5/4/26), not a stale anchor. The current price used in the debate ($299.14) is modestly above the mid-June tape (about $292.70 per multiple quote sources), so the stock-is-about-17%-above-fair-value gap is slightly overstated (closer to about 15%) and the near-all-time-highs framing should read as down about 11% from the split-adjusted 52-week high of $328. The re-rating is largely spent, but the name has come off the high. The $70-100M greenfield capacity figure is from the earnings-call commentary (the chief financial officer's prudently-invest-in-incremental-capacity remark plus Seeking Alpha call coverage), not the press-release body, and is correctly flagged in the dossier as unsanctioned.
skeptic_evidence: Powell Industries Q2 FY2026 press release (GlobeNewswire/IR, 5/4/26): revenue $296.6M +6%, net income $45.9M, diluted EPS $1.25 vs $1.27 prior-year (EPS DOWN on revenue UP), gross margin 29.6%, new orders $490M +97%, book-to-bill 1.7x, backlog $1.8B +33% YoY; cash & ST investments ~$545M, no debt, stockholders' equity $709.1M; 3-for-1 split effective 4/2/26.

Powell 8-K / press release (5/4/26): 'Subsequent to the end of the second quarter, the Company was awarded an additional mega data center order with a value exceeding $400 million related to a behind-the-meter design of on-site generation assets' — the largest order in company history; POST-QUARTER and public, confirming catalyst FIRED.

Seeking Alpha (5/4/26): 'Powell outlines $70M-$100M capacity option as it lands $400M+ data center mega order' — confirms the greenfield capacity expansion is an UNSANCTIONED option ('a decision within the next few quarters'), not a committed build; first-phase award has a 2-2.5 year burn.

Quote sources (mid-June 2026): POWL ~$292.70; split-adjusted 52-wk range $56.70-$328.00, all-time/52-wk high $328.00 reached 5/11/26 — re-rate fired post-award; stock now ~11% below the high, ~5x off the $56.70 low.

scale.role: Switchgear / electrical distribution for data centers + energy; >$400M record DC award, orders +97%, B2B 1.7x.
scale.rationale: Strong levered franchise with a fresh record >$400M behind-the-meter DC award (largest in company history) and orders +97%, but smaller and more cyclical (legacy oil/gas mix) than the grid CORE — a solid LEVER. Skeptic verdict CONFIRMED (every claim verified; catalyst FIRED, EPS actually fell to $1.25 on +6% revenue), so NO haircut — conviction reads at the full ~52 franchise. Re-rate largely spent (~11% off the split-adjusted high); wait.
scale.demand_durability: Medium-high — DC switchgear is bolting on fast, but legacy oil/gas/industrial mix dilutes the pure-AI durability.
